answers: Wrote /Users/kompzen/Offline/LLM-Graphrag/GraphRAG-chunksize/drift_trace_with_answer_20260223_203945.json FINAL ANSWER: Summary (short) - Based on the ticket notes, the FileZilla service is running on the VM named KF14azedi01 (10.10.10.10:21). Helpdesk ran client tests (WinSCP: FTP/SFTP) using account Ct_001680; Network performed a firewall analysis and the Core Team checked FileZilla site settings - but the ticket still lacks conclusive server logs / packet captures. The record also shows external FTP access was explicitly decommissioned (firewall policy removal) on 17.12.2024 [Data: Sources (34); Reports (3, 32, 93, 128)]. Likely diagnosis (prioritized) 1) Passive‑FTP data channel blocked / NAT misconfiguration (most likely): control (port 21) connects but the data connection (PASV ports) is not being established because passive ports are blocked or not NAT/forwarded. This is the classic cause when internal reachability is OK but external uploads hang. 2) FileZilla passive-mode or "masquerade/external IP" misconfiguration: server advertising an internal IP or an unforwarded port range in PASV replies. 3) Perimeter policy removal / deliberate decommissioning: ticket shows the external FTP policy was removed on 17.12.24 - if still in effect, external uploads will time out by design. 4) Lower probability: host‑side (Windows) firewall, FileZilla limits, or account/permission issues (would usually show different log symptoms). (These categories are the same prioritized checks recommended in the incident notes and summaries [Data: Sources (34); Reports (3, 32, 93)].) Next checks - ordered, actionable (what to run now) 1) Confirm ownership & service state - Who owns KF14azedi01 and can get server logs / run tcpdump? Ticket shows ownership/installer is unclear; confirm owner before making changes [Data: Sources (34); Reports (128)]. 2) Check whether external FTP is currently allowed - Ask Network to confirm whether the firewall/NAT rule that published FTP (external->10.10.10.10:21) exists or was removed (17.12.24 change is recorded). If it's absent, that explains the timeouts immediately [Data: Sources (34); Reports (93)]. 3) Collect server-side log + config (1st step for Helpdesk / 1st‑level) - Pull FileZilla Server logs covering the failing attempt(s); filter for username Ct_001680 and look for PASV/PORT replies and 425/426/timeout errors. Export FileZilla passive-mode settings (passive port range and "external IP / masquerade" setting) and netstat/ss output showing listening ports. (If you cannot access, escalate to the person who owns the VM.) 4) Reproduce and capture (coordinate with Network & Core) - Coordinate a short, scheduled reproduction window and simultaneously capture: - Perimeter/firewall pcap (ingress/egress) and firewall logs for the test window. - A pcap on the FTP VM (or its hypervisor host) capturing port 21 and the configured passive-range. - Ensure clocks/NTP are synchronized for accurate correlation. 5) Quick functional tests while capturing - From an external test host: connect to public IP:21, issue PASV and note the 227 reply. Compute data port (p1*256+p2). From that client try a TCP connect to that data port to test reachability. From an internal host run equivalent test to compare behavior. Exact artifacts to gather before escalation (minimum packet of evidence) - FileZilla Server log lines (timestamped) with the failing sessions (USER, PASV/PORT, 150/226/425/426 or TLS errors). - Server netstat/ss showing listeners (port 21 and passive-range). - Client-side WinSCP debug log for the failing attempt (with timestamps and client IP). - Perimeter firewall logs (allow/deny) and NAT/conntrack snapshot for the same timestamps. - PCAPs: perimeter interface and server NIC for the same reproduce window. If you need a concise list to hand to the admins, I can paste one you can copy/paste. What to ask Network (copy‑paste example) "Please produce a pcap on the Internet‑facing and LAN‑facing firewall interfaces for YYYY‑MM‑DD HH:MM -> HH:MM UTC, full snaplen (-s 0), and provide firewall logs filtered for dst=10.10.10.10 and dst ports 21 and <passive-port-range>. Also export the NAT/conntrack snapshot for the same window and confirm whether the external FTP publish rule exists or was removed." (If you want, I'll format a ready-to-send message and include the passive-port-range filter once you confirm the server setting.) Ticket level: 1st‑level or 2nd‑level? - Start as 1st‑level (Helpdesk) to gather the artifacts listed above (FileZilla logs, netstat, client WinSCP logs, exact failing timestamps and client IP). The incident notes explicitly advise that 1st‑line should collect logs and capture evidence before escalating [Data: Sources (34); Reports (3)]. - Escalate to 2nd‑level (Network / Core Engineering) when either: - Evidence shows control channel works but data‑channel SYNs never reach the server (network/NAT/firewall problem), or - You lack privileges to collect pcap / firewall NAT state or to change NAT/firewall rules, or - Server logs show binding/passive‑port issues that require server‑owner intervention. The ticket history recommends precisely this flow: 1st‑level collects and 2nd‑level acts on firewall/NAT or privileged server changes [Data: Sources (34); Reports (3)]. Short checklist / next 60‑minute plan 1. Confirm owner and authorization to run captures. 2. Helpdesk: pull FileZilla logs (±15 min around reported failure), netstat, and get the user's WinSCP log and client public IP. 3. Coordinate a 5-10 minute reproduce window with Network and Core. 4. Network: capture perimeter pcap + export NAT/session table for the window. Core: capture server pcap and tail FileZilla logs. 5. Correlate: look for PASV 227 reply IP (internal vs public) and whether client SYNs to the PASV port are translated and forwarded to 10.10.10.10. Brief protocol note (general knowledge) - FTP (PASV) uses a control channel on 21 and separate ephemeral server ports for data; NAT/firewall must allow and/or rewrite the PASV reply and forward the passive port range. If the server advertises a private IP in PASV replies or the passive ports are not forwarded, external uploads will hang while internal clients succeed [Data: General Knowledge (https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc959)]. If you want, I will: - Draft the exact capture commands/log grep lines for your team (tcpdump/tshark, grep/PowerShell) and a ready‑to‑send Network request email, or - Review the artifacts you collect (pcap + FileZilla logs + firewall logs) and point to the exact failure line(s). Key data anchors I used above (for your traceability) - Ticket and troubleshooting notes identifying the host (KF14azedi01 / 10.10.10.10:21), test account Ct_001680, WinSCP/FTP/SFTP tests, Network/Core involvement and recorded firewall policy removal on 17.12.2024 [Data: Sources (34); Reports (3, 32, 93, 128)]. Tell me what level of access you have (server console / firewall admin / only helpdesk) and I'll produce the exact commands and a copy‑paste request for Network/Core.
